title: Quantum Solvers for Nonlinear Matrix Equations in Quantum Chemistry
authors: Pablo Rodenas-Ruiz, Andrew Zhao, Joonho Lee
abstract: We present a quantum algorithm for solving algebraic Riccati equations, with applications to quantum-chemical random-phase approximation (RPA) and higher-order RPA theories. Our method block-encodes stabilizing Riccati solutions via Riesz projectors onto invariant subspaces of an associated non-normal matrix, implemented using contour-integral resolvents and quantum singular value transformations. Applied to $m$-particle, $m$-hole RPA, our algorithm yields a block-encoding of the amplitude solution and estimates the electronic correlation-energy density with it. Under localized-orbital sparsity assumptions, the end-to-end cost scales linearly with system size and polynomially with excitation rank $m$, suggesting an exponential advantage in $m$ over plausible classical local-correlation heuristics. More broadly, this work provides a framework for quantum algorithms for nonlinear matrix equations in quantum chemistry and opens a possible route toward developing quantum algorithms for coupled-cluster theory.
summary: This work presents a quantum algorithm to solve the Algebraic Riccati Equation relevant to calculating electronic correlation energy in quantum chemistry. By block-encoding solutions using Riesz projectors and QSVT, the authors demonstrate a quantum speedup in scaling with the excitation rank $m$. This establishes a general quantum framework for nonlinear matrix equations in quantum chemistry, potentially applicable to coupled-cluster theory.
main_problem: Developing a quantum algorithm to solve nonlinear matrix equations, specifically the Algebraic Riccati Equation (CARE), arising in quantum chemistry.
main_result: The algorithm achieves a computational cost scaling linearly with system size and polynomially with excitation rank $m$, suggesting an exponential advantage over classical methods for calculating correlation energy.
method: The method block-encodes stabilizing Riccati solutions using Riesz projectors constructed via contour-integral resolvents and implemented using Quantum Singular Value Transformations (QSVT).
model_or_system: The application is in quantum chemistry, specifically calculating the electronic correlation energy density using the Random-Phase Approximation (RPA) and its $m$-particle-$m$-hole generalizations ($m$-RPA).
key_observables: Electronic correlation-energy density ($E_c$), the stabilizing solution $X_s$, and the block-encoded Riesz projector ($\Pi_a$).
important_parameters: System size ($V$), excitation rank ($m$), spectral gap ($\delta$), and desired error ($\epsilon$).
main_assumptions: Assumes localized-orbital sparsity, that the associated matrix $H$ has no eigenvalues on the imaginary axis, and relies on the convergence of contour integrals via the trapezoid rule.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the workflow: (1) Block-encoding $\Pi_a$ via contour quadrature, (2) Extracting $X_s$ using $\Pi_1$ and $\Pi_2^+$, and (3) Calculating $	ext{Tr}(BX)$ using amplitude estimation.
paper_structure: The paper builds from defining the CARE and its associated Hamiltonian $H$, detailing the quantum machinery (Riesz projectors, QSVT, block-encoding), and finally applying this framework to derive the scaling for the correlation energy estimation.
why_interesting: It provides a concrete, quantum-algorithmic pathway to tackle notoriously difficult, non-linear many-body problems in quantum chemistry, bridging quantum computation directly to electronic structure theory.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
